title: Pigeon Toes
content:
В В В В A dusty clearing in the scrubs
В В В В Of barren, western lands, 
В В В В Where, out of sight, or sign of hope
В В В В The wretched school-house stands;
В В В В A roof that glares at glaring days,
В В В В A bare, unshaded wall,
В В В В A fence that guards no blade of green, 
В В В В A dust-storm over all.
В В В В The books and slates are packed away,
В В В В The maps are rolled and tied,
В В В В And for an hour I breathe, and lay
В В В В My ghastly mask aside;
В В В В I linger here to save my head
В В В В From voices shrill and thin,
В В В В That rasp for ever in the shed,
В В В В The пїЅhomeпїЅ IпїЅm boarding in.

В В В В The heat and dirt and wretchedness
В В В В With which their lives began, 
В В В В Bush mother nagging day and night,
В В В В And sullen, brooding man;
В В В В The minds that harp on single strings,
В В В В And never bright by chance,
В В В В The rasping voice of paltry things,
В В В В The hopeless ignorance.

В В В В I had ideals when I came here,
В В В В A noble purpose had,
В В В В But all that they can understand
В В В В Is пїЅaxe to grindпїЅ or пїЅmad.пїЅ
В В В В I brood at times till comes a fear
В В В В That sets my brain awhirl, 
В В В В I fight a strong manпїЅs battle here,
В В В В And I am but a girl.

В В В В I hated paltriness and deemed
В В В В A breach of faith a crime;
В В В В I listen now to scandalпїЅs voice
В В В В In sewing-lesson time.
В В В В There is a thought that haunts me so,
В В В В And gathers strength each day, 
В В В В Shall I as narrow-minded grow,
В В В В As mean of soul as they?

В В В В The feuds that rise from paltry spite,
В В В В Or from no cause at all;
В В В В The brooding, dark, suspicious minds, 
В В В В I suffer for it all.
В В В В They do not dream the пїЅTeacherпїЅ knows,
В В В В What brutal thoughts are said;
В В В В The children call me пїЅPigeon Toes,пїЅ
В В В В пїЅGreen EyesпїЅ and пїЅCarrot Head.пїЅ

В В В В On phantom seas of endless change
В В В В My thoughts to madness roam, 
В В В В The only thing that keeps me here,
В В В В The thoughts of those at home, 
В В В В The hearts that love and cling to me,
В В В В That I love best on earth,
В В В В My mother left in poverty,
В В В В My brother blind from birth.

В В В В On burning West Australian fields
В В В В In that great dreadful land,
В В В В Where all day long the heat waves flow
В В В В OпїЅer the seas of glowing sand.
В В В В My elder brother toils and breaks
В В В В That great true heart of his
В В В В To rescue us from poverty, 
В В В В To rescue me from this.

В В В В And one is with him where he goes,
В В В В My brotherпїЅs mate and mine;
В В В В He never called me Pigeon Toes, 
В В В В He said my eyes were пїЅfineпїЅ;
В В В В And his face comes before me now,
В В В В And hope and courage rise,
В В В В The lines of life, the troubled brow,
В В В В Firm mouth and kind grey eyes.

В В В В I preach content and gentleness,
В В В В And mock example give;
В В В В They little think the Teacher hates
В В В В And loathes the life they live.
В В В В I told the infants fairy tales
В В В В But half an hour since, 
В В В В They little dream how Pigeon Toes
В В В В Prays for a fairy Prince.

В В В В I have one prayer (and God forgive
В В В В A selfish prayer and wild);
В В В В I kneel down by the infantsпїЅ stool
В В В В (For I am but a child),
В В В В And pray as IпїЅve prayed times untold
В В В В That Heaven will set a sign,
В В В В To guide my brother to the gold,
В В В В For motherпїЅs sake and mine.

В В В В A dust cloud on the lonely road,
В В В В And I am here alone;
В В В В I lock the door till it be past,
В В В В For I have nervous grown.

В В В В God spare me disappointmentпїЅs blow.
В В В В He stops beside the gate;
В В В В A voice, thrill-feeling that I know.
В В В В My brother! No! His mate!

В В В В His eyes, a proud, triumphant smile,
В В В В His arms outstretched, and пїЅCome,
В В В В пїЅFor Jack and I have made our pile,
В В В В пїЅAnd IпїЅm here to take you homeпїЅ!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
